coredata.dc:title: Photoinduced holes transfer based visual determination of dopamine in human serum
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Kanagasubbulakshmi, S.
Kadirvelu, K.
coredata.dc:description: A simple unexplored strategy was followed to construct ratiometric fluorescence-based sensing system for the detection of dopamine (DA) in human serum. Ratiometric fluorescence system was constructed through bonding energy transfer (TBET) by conjugating carboxyl functionalized CdTe quantum dots (QDs) and amine-capped Carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The photophysical properties of sensing system were characterized by standard methods. Photoluminescence (PL) of sensing system under excitonic wavelength (350 nm) depends on dual emission at 440 and 595 nm that corresponds to CQDs and CdTe QDs respectively. The developed sensing system was utilized for visual determination of DA, an unquenched blue fluorescence of CQDs in ratiometric system reveals the visual color differentiation for DA binding with CdTe QDs among the possible interferences (Alanine, Glycine, Glucose, Sucrose, Urea and Ascorbic acid). The limit of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) was calculated as 8.1 and 27.2 nm respectively by using regression analysis. Photoinduced holes transfer (PHT) might have attributed the possible sensing mechanism for DA that quench the photoluminescence sequentially to enhance the sensing performance of QDs. The matrix interferences and reliability of the developed sensing platform were evaluated by testing DA spiked human serum and the sensing response was found to be field deployable.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Carbon dots
Dopamine
Ratiometric
Human serum
Visual detection

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and Functionalization of CdTe QDs
Detection of Dopamine by Ratiometric Fluorescent System
Systematic Characterization of Ratiometric Fluorescent Probe
Detection of Dopamine by Ratiometric Fluorescent Probe
On Field Evaluation of Developed Sensing Methodology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge DRDO funding support and JRF fellowship for S. Kanagasubbulakshmi.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic explanation of the ratiometric fluorescent system construction and detection mechanism of dopamine.

Characterization of the ratiometric fluorescent probe a) Emission spectrum of Carbon QDs b) Emission spectrum of CdTe QDs c) Emission spectrum of ratiometric fluorescent sensing system d) Absorbance spectrum of ratiometric sensing system (Inset a) CdTe QDs b) Carbon QDs c) ratiometric sensing system under UV illumination (365 nm).
HRTEM images of a) CQDs b) CdTe QDs c) Ratiometric sensing system d) Ratiometric sensing system + dopamine (Insets of each image shows the corresponding SAED pattern).
Infrared bonding patterns of the whole ratiometric sensing system.

Detection of dopamine by PL quenching spectrum a) CQDs b) CdTe QDs c) Ratiometric sensing system d) Absorbance spectrum the ratiometric sensing system in the presence and absence of dopamine (Inset a) CdTe QDs b) Carbon QDs c) ratiometric sensing system d) ratiometric sensing system +dopamine, under UV illumination (365 nm).

Evaluation of possible interferences effect in the sensing system a) Selective PL quenching efficiency of interferences b) PL quenching efficiency (I440/595) c) Fluorescence images of sensing system under UV illumination (365 nm) upon the addtdion of interferences.
Mechanism of PL quenching by dopamine interaction with CdTe QDs.
Comparison table of existing ratiometric sensing methods for dopamine.
Detection of Dopamine in human serum.

head.ce:title: Photoinduced holes transfer based visual determination of dopamine in human serum

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: DRDO-BU Center for Life Sciences, Bharathiar University Campus, Coimbatore 641046, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple unexplored strategy was followed to construct ratiometric fluorescence-based sensing system for the detection of dopamine (DA) in human serum. Ratiometric fluorescence system was constructed through bonding energy transfer (TBET) by conjugating carboxyl functionalized CdTe quantum dots (QDs) and amine-capped Carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The photophysical properties of sensing system were characterized by standard methods. Photoluminescence (PL) of sensing system under excitonic wavelength (350 nm) depends on dual emission at 440 and 595 nm that corresponds to CQDs and CdTe QDs respectively. The developed sensing system was utilized for visual determination of DA, an unquenched blue fluorescence of CQDs in ratiometric system reveals the visual color differentiation for DA binding with CdTe QDs among the possible interferences (Alanine, Glycine, Glucose, Sucrose, Urea and Ascorbic acid). The limit of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) was calculated as 8.1 and 27.2 nm respectively by using regression analysis. Photoinduced holes transfer (PHT) might have attributed the possible sensing mechanism for DA that quench the photoluminescence sequentially to enhance the sensing performance of QDs. The matrix interferences and reliability of the developed sensing platform were evaluated by testing DA spiked human serum and the sensing response was found to be field deployable.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) (3,4-dihydroxy phenyl ethylamine) has a significant role in the human body as an important neurotransmitter for catecholamine compound as a biomarker for many neurological disorders namely, Parkinson's Alzheimer's, Schizophrenia, and Huntington's diseases [1]. Abnormality of DA level in case of neurological disorders can lead to fluctuation in normal level of DA in human serum (0.1 μM) [2]. There are several methods specially reported on the detection of DA by using different nanomaterials, polymers and biomolecules based immunoassays [3]. The field applicability of detection methodologies still has been a difficult task due to the strong similarity of DA with ascorbic acid (AA) and uric acid (UA) and its coexistence in physiological fluids [4]. Therefore the development of efficient detection system for DA based on selective binding with the signal probe is essential.

Recently many studies have reported the detection of DA using single fluorescence signal based detection those have not addressed the selective binding of DA and role of interferences on the surface of the detection probes. Conventional PL based methodologies for the detection of targets have many disadvantages when it comes to real sample applications. The PL signals could fluctuate in the presence of possible interferences as they are working based on physical absorbance and direct interaction with materials [5]. Dual color emitting ratiometric sensors for the detection of ions and organic molecules draw significant attention in recent times as it can enable visual detection platform for the targeted analytes [6].

Ratiometric fluorescence-based detection methodology is relatively a current area that enables visual detection based on the target reaction with the signal probes, which can minimize the inference errors, environmental factors and enhances the selectivity and sensitivity of detection system [7]. Ratiometric fluorescent system has been exploited by using different organic and inorganic materials for the sensing of ascorbic acid [8], Bacillus thuringiensis transgenic sequence [9], Nitric acid [10], and metal ions [11]. In dual emission system, the mechanism of detection can occur through many strategies including internal charge transfer (ICT), excited-state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT),
fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), through-bond energy transfer

(TBET), and monomer-excimer formation [12]. In particular, TBET has no overlapping of emission spectrum that can give precise detection of targeted molecules. Designing of ratiometric sensing system has one donor responsible for target-specific binding that gets eventually quenched and another remains constant to enable the visual detection [13]. Photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and photoinduced hole transfer (PHT) mechanism also possible when the surface of QDs gets interacted with amine-based organic surfactants.

Currently, the reports for the detection of DA by using ratiometric fluorescent methodology have used organic molecules [14], carbon quantum dots (CQDs), semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [7], metal nanoparticles [15] fluorescent organic nanoparticles [16], rare earth doped upconversion nanoparticles [17] and taro carbon based electrochemical approach [18]. Though the reports were able to give visual detection platform for DA, few research gaps are there as mentioned below, 1. Existing reports have used linkers to connect the two fluorescent systems that can form a ratiometric probe instead of direct bonding. 2. Photostability of the ratiometric probes in case of organic molecules capping. 3. Charge based attraction towards the analyte by the ratiometric probe. 4. Role of AA and UA as possible interferences and mechanism of the selective binding with DA.

Here, the research gaps were taken to address the challenges in dopamine detection by developing ratiometric fluorescent based sensing system. Amine functionalized carbon dots and carboxyl functionalized CdTe QDs were synthesized and conjugated directly by using prominent experiment conditions. As fabricated ratiometric fluorescent sensing system was applied in dopamine detection along with possible interferences. On-field applicability of the developed detection methodology was evaluated using spiked human serum samples. Ratiometric fluorescent sensing system was able to enable visual detection platform by an immediate color change in the presence of dopamine. Mechanism of dopamine detection using ratiometric fluorescent sensing was elucidated with the help of PHT principles.

Cadmium oxide (CdO), Octadecene (ODE), Oleic acid (OA), Tellurium (Te), MPA (3-Mercaptopropionic acid), Citric acid, Urea, Dopamine hydrochloride, Alanine, Glycine, Glucose, Urea and Ascorbic acid Analar grade (AR) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich. Double distilled water was used for Phosphate buffered saline (PBS) buffer preparation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and Functionalization of CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: CdTe QDs were synthesized by using reflux condensation method with minor modifications as reported elsewhere [19]. Briefly, Oleic acid and Octadecene mixture was used to prepare Cadmium precursor at 180 °C. Octadecene was used to prepare Tellurium precursor. The equimolar ratio was maintained to form a strong core of CdTe QDs. Once the monomers of precursors are ready, the swift hot injection was followed to allow the nucleation at 120 °C. Once the reaction has completed the core QDs were suspended in toluene. Precipitation efficiency of commonly used solvents such as acetone, ethanol and methanol were analyzed. The fluorescence characteristics of QDs were investigated spectrofluorimetrically by dissolving each set of the samples in toluene and chloroform.

As synthesized CdTe core QDs have replaceable ligands on QDs surface [20]. Phase separated QDs were taken in chloroform and toluene contained vial for ligand exchange process with MPA. A fixed concentration and volume of MPA was added to the 1 mL of QDs solution and vortexed for 15 min. As separated two layers were centrifuged and washed with ethanol, thus obtained pellet was collected and redispersed in a pH 7 PBS for further studies [21].

The CQDs were synthesized by following previous reports with minor modifications. Briefly, the citric acid and urea were used as a major precursor to synthesize CQDs. Each 1 g of citric acid and urea were taken in 50 mL hydrothermal autoclave and treated at 180 °C for 6 h. Once the carbonization has completed the mixture was cooled and centrifuged at 15000 RPM for 15 min. The supernatant was collected and stored in 4 °C for further usage. The synthesized CQDs were capped with amine groups by using hydrothermal co-pyrolysis of citric acid and urea. [22,23]

The ratiometric fluorescent probe was developed by direct linking of carboxyl functionalized CdTe QDs and amine terminated CQDs. In this process, the condensation reaction between carboxyl and amine groups was achieved without EDC and NHS activation. In brief, 150 μL of carboxyl functionalized CdTe QDs and 350 μL of CQDs were allowed to react at room temperature for 1 h in alkaline condition (pH-9). As reported in previous studies the deprotonation nature of the COOH group of CdTe QDs in alkaline pH helped to form the amide linkage in ratiometric system [24]. Once the reaction has completed the solution was centrifuged to remove unreacted moieties and the pellet was dissolved in borate buffer (pH 8) for further usage. The slightly alkaline condition was maintained to fabricate the sensitive sensing platform towards dopamine as it increases the rate of oxidation. [25]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Dopamine by Ratiometric Fluorescent System
ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine concentration was fixed in the range of 10–100 nM and added by standard addition method in the sensing system. The solution was characterized by spectrofluorometer and zetasizer to analyze the sensing parameters. The possible interferences (Alanine, Glycine, Glucose, Sucrose, Urea and Ascorbic acid) were added along with DA and the PL response was recorded. The concentration of each compound was maintained as 1 mM to obtain a maximum signal from the sensing system. Human serum was collected from Kovai medical college hospital, Coimbatore, Tamilnadu, India and DA was spiked by standard addition method to analyze the field efficacy of the developed sensing system.

The synthesized materials were characterized to confirm the photophysical and chemical properties. Absorbance spectra were recorded using a UV-Vis-NIR spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU 3600 model). Emission spectra were recorded using a Spectrofluorometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluromax 4). Hydrodynamic radius and Zeta potential were analyzed using ZETASIZER MALVERN MAKE. Surface morphology, the size of QDs and SAED pattern were examined using HR-TEM (JOEL JEM 2100 at 200KV). To identify bonding patterns, the infrared spectra were taken using FT-IR (SHIMADZU 8400S model).

Development and possible chemistry behind the construction of ratiometric fluorescent sensing system using CQDs and CdTe QDs are illustrated in Fig. 1 . The mechanism of DA binding with the ratiometric fluorescent system and color changing under UV illumination is also pictured in the scheme. Ratiometric probe was constructed based on amide bonding between amine and carboxyl group of QDs directly that avoids additional linkers to support conjugation [26]. The protonation states of dopamine's 2-hydroxyl groups (pKa values 9.3, 12.6) also change as a function of pH, directly affecting electrochemistry. At pH 8 the dopamine undergoes a proportional increase in oxidation by ambient O2 and the appearance of this electron acceptor near the CdTe QDs provides a favourable non radiative channel for increased quenching of CdTe QDs QDs [27,28]. Unlike other existing reports, this new strategy has followed the TBET principle that prevents defragmentation of donor and acceptor and gives enhanced detection limit of an analyte. Here energy transfer is occurring through the bonding between target (NH2) and analyte (COOH) without spectral overlapping. [29]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Systematic Characterization of Ratiometric Fluorescent Probe
ce:section.ce:para: Dual-emission probe was constructed using amine functionalized CQDs and carboxyl functionalized CdTe QDs directly in alkaline pH with the help of amide bond formation. In those CQDs has served as strong reference signal provider and CdTe QDs as a detection probe to interact with the targeted analyte. The ratio of CQDs and CdTe QDs was maintained as 2:1 to enable the strong visual determination of dopamine. Ratiometric fluorescent sensing system was characterized to evaluate the photophysical properties (Fig. 2 ). In that, CQDs have exhibited strong blue emission at 440 nm whereas CdTe QDs have given emission at 595 nm that corresponds to a yellowish orange color. On another hand, the ratiometric system has provided both emission peaks under single excitonic wavelength (350 nm) that proves the TBET principle and without spectral overlapping. Absorbance spectrum of sensing system has shown in Fig. 2d that confirms the ratiometric formation of QDs. As shown in Fig. 2d, CQDs have two absorption bands: one located at 240 nm likely originates from the formation of multiple polyaromatic chromophores, and the other one centred at 350 nm is ascribed to the n-π* transition of CDs. [22,30] The appearance of shouldered peak at 550 nm that represent CdTe QDs is given as a magnified image as it has been supressed by the existence of CQDs. Hydrodynamic radius (RH) was measured in order to confirm the size of sensing system (Fig. S1). RH of the CdTe QDs, CQDs and CdTe + CQDs was 3.5 nm, 7 nm and 15 nm respectively, increase in RH of the whole system has taken as a preliminary confirmation for ratiometric formation of QDs.

Morphological and crystallinity characteristics were analyzed by HR-TEM combined SAED patterns (Fig. 3 ). Synthesized QDs were found to be uniform in size (CQDs-5 nm, CdTe QDs-3 nm) and spherical in structure under HR-TEM. Construction of sensing system was visualized in images that represent the formation of dual emissive QDs by nano-bridges without adhering to the surfaces on each other. Size and uniformity of the QDs have been maintained in the ratiometric formation through amide bridges.

Lattice parameters were derived from selective area energy diffraction (SAED) pattern for each quantum dots and whole sensing system. Amorphous nature of CQDs by diffused ring patterns [31] was confirmed from Fig. 3a (inset). The crystalline structure of CdTe QDs (Fig. 3b inset) was found to be cubic zinc blende by (311), (220), (111) hkl values (JCPS PDF 65-1046) [32]. Ratiometric sensing system has maintained the same crystallinity (Fig. 3c inset) but the intensity of the ring was found to be less due to the presence of CQDs. SAED pattern of the DA interaction with sensing system (Fig. 3d inset) resembles SAED pattern of CQDs since CdTe QDs has agglomerated after the addition of DA. Here, we speculate the agglomeration of QDs was occurred in the presence of DA as it has interacted with the COOH groups on the CdTe QDs, which reduces the distance between neighbouring particles and leads to agglomeration. A similar mechanism is reported in previous studies those have enabled the efficient sensing platform [5,33,34].

Infrared (IR) bonding patterns of the whole system have illustrated in Fig. 4 . Carboxyl and amine groups play a vital role in the conjugation of QDs as well as in detection of dopamine. Since the CQDs were synthesized by co-pyrolysis of citric acid and urea, that has given the significant stretching vibrations of COOH as well as NH stretching in FT-IR analysis. The above justification was verified with existing literatures that report similar phenomena in FT-IR studies [22,35].

These groups on the surface of QDs were confirmed by significant stretching and bending vibrations in IR bonding patterns. Stretching vibrations at 3300 cm−1 and 1650 cm−1 is attributed to CO conjugated OH for carboxyl groups of the QDs. Bending vibration at 1656 cm−1 for NH confirms the presence of amine groups on the CQDs. A shifted vibration at 3400 and 1704 cm−1 in ratiometric system confirms the strong amide bridge between CQDs and CdTe QDs. However the unbounded COOH groups stretching's of the CdTe QDs were found to be significant in terms of the ratiometric sensing platform development for DA. A broad stretching vibration at 3380 cm−1 is attributed C conjugated NH that represent the DA interaction with ratiometric sensing system and shifted stretching vibration at 1640 and 1212 denotes the CO and CN groups of the ratiometric sensing system beyond the interaction with DA [36,37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Dopamine by Ratiometric Fluorescent Probe
ce:section.ce:para: In order to find target specific sensing of DA, both systems were analyzed by spectrofluorometer and the PL intensity of sensing system is illustrated in Fig. 5 . Standard addition of DA to sensing system caused systematic PL quenching of CdTe QDs at the same time the blue emission of CQDs was remained stable (Fig. 5a & b). Ratiometric sensing system was evaluated for its sensitivity against DA by measuring PL intensity. The concentration-dependent emission spectrum of sensing probe reveals the more specific interaction of DA with carboxyl functionalized QDs than CQDs (Fig. 5c). Sensing medium pH was maintained slightly alkaline (pH 8) by considering the practical application for the detection of DA in human serum.

Corresponding absorbance spectrum for the sensing system and interaction of DA with sensing system has given in Fig. 5d. The strong decrement in absorbance of CdTe QDs and appearance of a new peak at 280 nm confirms the interaction of DA. It is also evident from the results, the agglomeration of CdTe QDs enables the visual determination of DA [38]. Concentration (10–100 nM) dependent PL quenching efficiency by DA was plotted separately to each wavelength to evaluate the concept with Stern- Volmer plot (Eq. 1, Fig. S1).
(3)
f o
/ f = 1 +
K sv
C q
where, fo, f is the PL intensity of QDs in the absence and presence of DA respectively, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant and Cq is the concentration of DA. PL quenching can be deduced into static or dynamic from the quenching spectrum that depends on the interaction of an analyte. Here, PL intensity of the QDs has decreased systematically upon the addition of analyte that evident static quenching efficiency of DA. [39] LOD and LOQ were calculated using standard formula as 8.1 and 27.2 nm respectively by regression analysis method that corresponds to the concentration of DA [24].

Matrix interference analysis is the main parameter in the development of field deployable sensing system. Here, the possible and major interference (Alanine, Glycine, Glucose, Sucrose, Urea and Ascorbic acid) were selected to evaluate the selectivity of ratiometric sensing towards DA. PL quenching spectra of sensing system were taken along with the possible interferences (concentration-1 mM) that show the maximum quenching ability of DA among other molecules (Fig. 6a). Quenching efficiency of each analyte was analyzed by PL intensity ratio (I440/595) that has shown the great variation to each interfrence molecules (Fig. 6b). Effect of interferences molecules in the fluorescence of sensing system has been well correlated with the PL emission spectrum (Fig. 6c). From the results, the maximum PL quenching ability was obtained for DA along with interferences that indicates the sensing probe exhibits good selectivity and specificity for DA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: On Field Evaluation of Developed Sensing Methodology
ce:section.ce:para: Field applicability of the developed sensing methodology was evaluated by detecting trace amount of DA in human serum. Generally, the existing enzyme-free detection methodologies have demonstrated DA detection limit up to μM concentration from the field samples (Table 1). Here, the PHT based sensing methodology was able to give LOD up to 8.21 nM that is essential in case of nervous disorders and various diseases. Recovery experiments were performed by standard addition of DA into diluted human serum and the recovery percentage range was obtained 98 to 116 with less than 8% relative standard deviations (Table 2 ). Addition DA contained human serum to ratiometric sensing system has enabled the visual determination of DA (Fig. S4). From the results, it can be concluded that as developed ratiometric sensing system can be successfully used to detect and monitor the DA level in various biomedical applications.

In this study, ratiometric sensing system was constructed by using TBET principles that helps large stoke shifted donor and acceptor molecules to enable the visual detection platform. Novelty aspects of this study include the following factors, 1. Conjugation of CQDs and CdTe QDs without additional linkers supports by optimizing pH of the reaction medium 2. SAED pattern based confirmation to the formation of ratiometric sensing system 3. PHT based sensing mechanism for specific interaction of DA among possible interferences 4. Enhanced sensitivity was achieved up to nanomolar level detection of DA in human serum 5. Linker and enzyme-free sensing platform provide a photostable system for effective detection of DA among the matrix of interferences. The surface charge based explanation for the ratiometric construction and interaction of DA with sensing system was found to be a strong support for PHT mechanism. Overall, the developed methodology has proven its efficacy in DA detection directly in human serum and can be taken forward to develop a point of care detection in biomedical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge DRDO funding support and JRF fellowship for S. Kanagasubbulakshmi.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2018.08.050.
